We saw an unprecedented surge in interest in food growing when the pandemic started, with our customers and community reporting huge benefits to their mental health and overall well-being. As we look forward, the climate crisis will become an even greater test of our resilience. Food growing has a unique way of connecting people with the planet and will be a powerful tool to mobilise positive environmental action in the years ahead. The success we’ve had in recent years would not have been possible without the Growth Fund support, and the impact will be felt even more in the years to come (Michael Kelly,CEO, GIY)

The main challenge is sustaining engagement and ownership from players in their mental health journey. While awareness is rising, consistent help-seeking behaviours and embedding wellbeing as a core part of development remain uneven. Additionally, resources are finite so we need to balance the breadth of issues (acute mental health needs, transition, brain health, neurodiversity, everyday wellbeing) with the depth of support we can realistically provide.

Young people from a care-experienced background are more likely to experience poor mental health outcomes due to the trauma and instability they have faced in their lives. This has been further complicated by covid-19 and lockdowns which in many cases had a negative impact on relationships, prevented young people from socializing outside of the home, and increased mental health complaints such as anxiety and depression. On the other hand, young people are more likely than ever to be assessed for neurodivergence or learning difficulties, which allows them to gain access to support earlier.
In my opinion, ensuring that mental health support is available to the people who need them most urgently. We need to remove as many barriers as possible for people accessing support when in crisis.

The main focus of our programmes is on encouraging movement and getting people, particularly teenagers, active. The goal is to improve both mental and physical well-being by encouraging them to get off their phones and out of the house. This includes specific initiatives like the "Outdoor Week" and "Teenagers in Sports" week.
The funding for these programmes is allocated by Sport Ireland, which provides money that is then distributed to support the initiatives.
Overall, the programmes operate under the broader banners of well-being and inclusion, which also encompass other initiatives like "women in sports."

The more important thing is the programs through the surf clubs, engagement, participation, getting people outside. It's not all about, you know, being a competitive surfer. It's that's actually the way smaller part of the picture. The main picture is the inclusion, getting people activated in the sport, and they don't have to take it up to a high level. If we can create lifelong participants, rather than competitive participants, would actually be our goal.

The main challenge is always money. We need more funding to run more programs and increase participation. This would allow us to give clubs more coaches, so they can run bigger programs and engage more people.

Volunteers are also a big problem. It's hard to get more volunteers. The existing pool is quite small, and they get exhausted. Drop-off is high, and retention is low. So, the question is how do you activate and retain people for the long term? We've tried creating a new pathway for coaches to alleviate this, but it's tough because people often lose interest and walk away.

So, it really comes down to retention being hard, both for participants and volunteers, along with the constant need for more funding for all the programs, most of which rely on volunteers anyway.

The main challenge in the context of school mental health right now is bridging the gap between rising needs and the capacity of schools to respond effectively.
On one side, more young people are experiencing anxiety, stress, and low wellbeing, with issues often presenting earlier and more intensely. On the other, schools face limits in time, resources, and specialist supports — meaning teachers, though willing, often feel under-prepared and overburdened. Long waiting lists for external services like CAMHS/NEPS add pressure, leaving schools to carry much of the responsibility. The challenge is to ensure timely, preventative, and strengths-based supports that are embedded in the curriculum, guided by clear frameworks, and resourced with proper training and professional input.

I believe the main challenge is poverty and its knock on effects to our students.
We have students who are hungry and unhygienic. We have many families battling addiction and anti social behaviour in their homes and surrounding areas. The trauma of growing up in this area impacts the majority of our students in many different ways from neglect to witnessing crime up to and including murder.

And the school refusal is the other, I mean that is a big… huge challenge for us and I suppose that is a resource issue in terms of the number of hours we would need to go out, pick a young person up, nurse them into school, support them through school.

At the moment, for us, working with young people I suppose particularly since post-covid, there is a larger awareness of issues around mental health for young people. Post-covid we would have supported a lot of young people who would have isolated themselves. School refusal would be the biggest issue, high anxiety, a lot of parental separation, domestic violence in the home that has impacted young people.

We find that social media has played a significant role in the lives of young people and their mental health. You know, they are looking at their friends enjoying themselves, they’re isolated, they are looking at how they look, how they perceive themselves, body dysmorphia, depression, anxiety.

For me, the two biggest issues we are facing with mental health are school refusal and anxiety. Anxiety in particular and it manifests itself in lots of different ways for young people. For some young people it is anxiety, even anxious to go join a group, anxious to go into school, just general anxiety about how they look, how they feel, how they feel they should look and feel. It is just such an anxious world for young people.

I could say that most students here would benefit from therapeutic services as Dublin 17 is one of the most disadvantaged areas in Ireland.
Personally, I see a lot of students struggling with anxiety. We also combat severe poverty and addiction.

I thought your suggestion for group work was a great idea and hopefully we can get that going soon. I really believe that your age bracket should come down to 12 as we have some first year students here that would really benefit from an early intervention due to their extreme life circumstances.

It goes back to the basics of how they are parented, are they encouraged? are they praised? do they feel loved? do they feel safe? And I think it is an environment of not feeling safe, not feeling appreciated, not feeling loved, and it is trying to go back to the basics of just put the phone down.

One big area for us, I suppose, with anxiety is when we explore it with young people is that sleep hygiene is very very poor. We had a group in Ballina recently and we did a two week experiment with them around their sleep and a lot of young people are getting 3-4 hours of sleep a night.

I think it's really important for everybody to understand that no matter what you're struggling with or what you're doing really well with at any time, everything is on the spectrum and on any given day I can present as having multiple personalities or being a manic depressive and other days being like really, really high and it's actually just normalising that we're not supposed to feel better all the time. We just need to get better at feeling so.
